critic_raw:
CT acquisition heterogeneity — scanner manufacturer, kVp, slice thickness, reconstruction kernel — is not addressed beyond Hounsfield normalization. The CNN may learn scanner-specific artifacts rather than pathology features if training data is scanner-imbalanced.

Architecture selection across three substantially different architectures on the validation set creates multiplicity risk — best validation performance may overfit to validation idiosyncrasies.

Sensitivity without specificity or AUROC as the primary metric cannot distinguish a clinically useful model from a degenerate all-positive classifier.
all_issues_raised: CT acquisition heterogeneity (scanner model, kVp, slice thickness, reconstruction kernel) is not addressed beyond Hounsfield normalization; the CNN may learn scanner-specific artifacts rather than pathology features if training data is scanner-imbalanced.

Architecture selection on the validation set across three substantially different architectures (ResNet-50, 3D CNN, ViT) creates multiplicity risk — the best validation architecture may overfit to validation idiosyncrasies.

Sensitivity is reported as the primary metric without specificity or AUROC; a degenerate classifier achieving 100% sensitivity by predicting all-positive cannot be distinguished from a clinically useful model.

round_transcripts.critic:
CT acquisition heterogeneity — scanner manufacturer, kVp, slice thickness, reconstruction kernel — is not addressed beyond Hounsfield normalization. The CNN may learn scanner-specific artifacts rather than pathology features if training data is scanner-imbalanced.

Architecture selection across three substantially different architectures on the validation set creates multiplicity risk — best validation performance may overfit to validation idiosyncrasies.

Sensitivity without specificity or AUROC as the primary metric cannot distinguish a clinically useful model from a degenerate all-positive classifier.
round_transcripts.defender:
Hounsfield unit normalization is the standard preprocessing for clinical CT analysis. Additional scanner calibration requires phantom data that is typically unavailable in retrospective archives. The institutional stratification in the split ensures that multiple acquisition environments are represented in each partition — this is the practical approach to acquisition heterogeneity in retrospective CT studies.

Architecture selection on validation data with three candidate architectures is standard neural architecture search practice. Three architectures do not constitute problematic multiplicity — this is equivalent to choosing between three hyperparameter settings, not a fishing expedition across hundreds of configurations. The test set remains untouched.

Sensitivity is the primary metric because the hypothesis is specifically about detection sensitivity — whether the CNN detects early-stage lung cancer at the rate of radiologist consensus. Specificity and AUROC are secondary metrics that provide additional context. The hypothesis does not claim the CNN replaces radiologists; it claims the CNN achieves comparable or superior sensitivity. Evaluating this claim requires sensitivity as the primary metric.

round_transcripts.critic:
The institutional stratification partially addresses scanner heterogeneity if each institution uses consistent equipment. The practical limitation of retrospective data is acknowledged. This concern is partially resolved but warrants explicit reporting of scanner distribution across splits.

The three-architecture selection on validation is adequately defended as standard practice — this concern is resolved.

Sensitivity as the primary metric for the stated hypothesis is adequately defended — the hypothesis is specifically about detection capability. This concern is resolved.
round_transcripts.defender: Scanner distribution reporting is a descriptive analysis enhancement that any responsible implementation would include. The design is methodologically sound for the stated hypothesis.

all_issues_adjudicated: CT acquisition heterogeneity (scanner model, kVp, slice thickness, reconstruction kernel) is not addressed beyond Hounsfield normalization; the CNN may learn scanner-specific artifacts rather than pathology features if training data is scanner-imbalanced.
